title: Paste paper comb patterns
subtitle: Drawing repeating patterns through coloured starch paste on paper
excerpt: Paste paper is made by painting coloured starch paste directly onto paper, then drawing combs, fingers, and shaped tools through the paste while it is still wet. The resulting patterns range from simple parallel stripes to complex shells, waves, and herringbone designs. Unlike carrageenan marbling, paste paper requires no mordanting and no special bath.
sourceNotes: Combed paste paper appears as bookbinding endpaper from at least the seventeenth century in Nuremberg and Augsburg binderies, where the German name Kleisterpapier was coined. Esther K. Smith, How to Make Books (Potter Craft, 2007), paste-paper chapter. Gabriele Mazzotta, Paste Papers (Gremese, 1994), historical paste paper styles from German and Italian binding workshops.

content.text: Combed paste paper is a sheet brushed with thick coloured paste, then combed in patterns while the paste is still wet. The German name for it is Kleisterpapier. The patterns are some of the most varied in the decorated-paper tradition: the comb teeth carve through the paste and leave a crisp ridge along every line.

Cook wheat-paste to a thick, smooth consistency: combine 2 tablespoons of plain flour with 200 ml of cold water, then heat slowly while stirring until the mixture thickens and turns translucent. Allow to cool. The paste should hold a peak but still spread easily with a wide brush. Add colour by stirring in a small amount of watercolour or gouache mixed with a few drops of water, start with a concentration that produces clearly saturated colour.

Lightly dampen the sheet on both sides with a sponge and clean water. A damp sheet accepts paste evenly without the paste dragging on dry fibres. Pin or tape the sheet to a board at all four corners, the paste will cause unsupported paper to cockle severely as it dries.
Spread a thick, even coat of coloured paste over the entire sheet with a wide brush. Then draw the comb through the wet paste in parallel passes from top to bottom to make

. For a wave pattern, draw the comb in a gentle sinusoidal curve rather than a straight line. For a shell or fan pattern, bring multiple curved strokes from the same starting point. For a herringbone, alternate the comb direction at 45° in each row.

For a two-colour paste paper, apply and comb the first colour. Let it dry partially for 10 minutes, until the surface is matt but still slightly flexible. Apply the second colour over the top. Comb at 90° or on a diagonal. The first colour shows through the second wherever the comb teeth remove paste from the ridges.

Leave pinned to the board until completely dry, at least two hours at room temperature. Peel carefully from the corners inward. The dried paste paper may be slightly stiff and slightly glossy. It can be flattened completely by placing between damp blotters under heavy boards for 30 minutes once the surface has cured.
